title: Shorbat adas
subtitle: Lebanese lentil soup with lemon and crispy onion
excerpt: Red lentils simmered until completely dissolved into a golden, velvety soup flavoured with cumin, turmeric, and a generous squeeze of lemon. Finished with a scattering of crispy fried onion and a drizzle of olive oil. One of the most nourishing and quickly made soups in the Levantine kitchen — ready in 35 minutes from pantry ingredients.
sourceNotes: Shorbat adas (lentil soup) is a staple of Levantine cooking across Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, and Jordan. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) documents it as one of the foundational soups of the Arab culinary tradition. The red lentil version is the most common in Lebanon. The crispy onion topping is standard and should not be omitted.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the soup without the onion topping for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch easily. Lentil soup keeps for 4 days in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the soup ahead and refrigerate. Reheat and fry the onion fresh just before serving.

content.text: Red lentils dissolve completely in about 20 to 25 minutes of simmering, producing a naturally smooth, thick soup without any blending. The texture that results is velvety and uniform. Cumin and turmeric are the key spices — cumin provides warmth and depth, turmeric gives the characteristic yellow colour. The lemon goes in at the end, squeezed generously: the soup should taste distinctly lemony.

The crispy onion on top is essential, not optional. Thinly sliced onion fried until deep brown and slightly crisp adds textural contrast and a concentrated sweetness that balances the lemony soup. Some versions also add a thin dribble of cumin-scented oil across the surface before serving.

items.prepNote: 1 finely chopped for the soup, 2 thinly sliced for the topping

content.text: Heat 3 tablespoons of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large pot. Cook the chopped {{onion}} for 8 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{turmeric}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{lentils-red}} and 1.2 litres of water. Bring to the boil, reduce to a simmer, and cook for 20 to 25 minutes until the lentils have completely dissolved. Season with salt. Add the {{lemon}} juice. Taste and adjust — the soup should be distinctly lemony and warmly spiced.

While the soup cooks, heat the remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the sliced {{onion}} and fry for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deep brown and crispy at the edges. Drain on kitchen paper. Ladle the soup into bowls, scatter the crispy onion over the top, and drizzle with a little extra olive oil.

items.cause: Too much water or lentils not fully dissolved
items.fix: Cook uncovered for 5 more minutes to reduce. Red lentils should dissolve completely — if not, they may need longer. The soup should be thick enough to coat a spoon

items.symptom: Soup is too thick and stiff
items.cause: Too little water or cooked too long after lentils dissolved
items.fix: Add water a little at a time, stirring between additions. The soup continues to thicken as it sits — add water when reheating

items.cause: Not enough lemon juice or added too early and cooked off
items.fix: Add lemon juice at the very end, off the heat. Taste and add more — the soup should be distinctly sharp. Two full lemons is not too much

items.symptom: Onions are soft rather than crispy
items.cause: Heat too low or too much oil
items.fix: Raise the heat to medium-high for the last 3 to 4 minutes. The onions need a final burst of heat to crisp up at the edges

content.text: Shorbat adas is the soup that Lebanese cooks make when there is nothing else in the house, a pantry soup requiring only lentils, an onion, and some spices. It is eaten at every level of Lebanese society, from restaurant starters to family weeknight dinners. The lemon is what distinguishes the Lebanese version from the Egyptian (which uses vinegar) and the Syrian (which is often unseasoned with citrus). In Beirut during the winter months, shorbat adas is a staple in the way that chicken soup is a staple in Central Europe — comforting, restorative, made when someone is ill or when the weather demands it.
